dependencies.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$, \[ (a+b)+c=a+(b+c). \]

dependencies.title: Addition Is Commutative
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b \in P$, \[ a+b=b+a. \]

dependencies.title: Addition Successor on the Right
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $m,n \in P$, \[ m+S(n)=S(m+n). \]

dependencies.title: Addition With $1$
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $m \in P$, \[ m+1=S(m). \]

dependencies.title: Every Element Is Either $1$ or a Successor
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $x \in P$, either $x=1$ or there exists $u \in P$ such that $S(u)=x$.

ancestor_tree.title: Cauchy Sequence with a Convergent Subsequence
ancestor_tree.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a Cauchy real sequence. If a subsequence of $(x_n)$ converges to $L\in\mathbb{R}$, then $(x_n)$ converges to $L$.

dependencies.title: Cauchy Sequence
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ is a \textbf{Cauchy sequence} if \[ \forall \varepsilon>0 \;\exists N\in\mathbb{N}\;\forall m,n\ge N \;\bigl(|x_m-x_n|<\varepsilon\bigr). \]

dependencies.title: Sequence
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $X$ be a nonempty set. A \textbf{sequence} in $X$ is a function \[ x : \mathbb{N} \to X. \] The value $x(n)$ is written $x_n$ and called the \textbf{$n$-th term} of the sequence. The sequence itself is denoted $(x_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$, or simply $(x_n)$ when the index set is clear.

dependencies.title: Function
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $A$ and $B$ be sets. A \emph{function} from $A$ to $B$ is a relation $f \subseteq A \times B$ such that: \begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)] \item (\emph{Existence / left-total}) for every $a \in A$, there exists $b \in B$ with $(a,b) \in f$; \item (\emph{Uniqueness / right-unique}) if $(a,b_1) \in f$ and $(a,b_2) \in f$ then $b_1 = b_2$. \end{enumerate}...

dependencies.title: Cartesian Product — as foundation for relations
dependencies.statement_preview: The \emph{Cartesian product} of sets $A$ and $B$ is: \[ A \times B \;:=\; \{\, (a,b) \mid a \in A \text{ and } b \in B \,\}. \]

dependencies.title: Axiom of Pairing
dependencies.statement_preview: For any two sets, there exists a set containing exactly those two sets. \[ \forall A \, \forall B \, \exists C \, \forall x \, (x \in C \leftrightarrow (x = A \lor x = B)). \]

dependencies.title: Axiom of Power Set
dependencies.statement_preview: For any set, there exists a set of all its subsets. \[ \forall A \, \exists P \, \forall x \, (x \in P \leftrightarrow x \subseteq A). \]

dependencies.title: Axiom Schema of Separation
dependencies.statement_preview: Given a set and a property, there exists a subset containing exactly the elements satisfying that property. For any formula $\varphi(x)$, \[ \forall A \, \exists B \, \forall x \, (x \in B \leftrightarrow (x \in A \land \varphi(x))). \]

dependencies.title: Ordered Pair
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $a$ and $b$ be sets. The \emph{ordered pair} $(a,b)$ is defined (Kuratowski) as \[ (a,b) \;:=\; \{\{a\}, \{a,b\}\}. \]

dependencies.title: Relation
dependencies.statement_preview: A \emph{relation} from $A$ to $B$ is any subset $R \subseteq A \times B$. If $(a,b) \in R$ we write $a \, R \, b$ and say ``$a$ is related to $b$.'' When $A = B$ we say $R$ is a \emph{relation on $A$}.

dependencies.title: Cartesian Product — as foundation for relations
dependencies.statement_preview: The \emph{Cartesian product} of sets $A$ and $B$ is: \[ A \times B \;:=\; \{\, (a,b) \mid a \in A \text{ and } b \in B \,\}. \]

dependencies.title: Subset
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $A$ and $B$ be sets. We say $A$ is a \emph{subset} of $B$, written $A \subseteq B$, if every element of $A$ is also an element of $B$: \[ A \subseteq B \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \forall x \, (x \in A \rightarrow x \in B). \]

dependencies.title: Strict Order Successor Characterization
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b \in P$, \[ a<b \Longleftrightarrow S(a) \le b. \]

dependencies.title: Less Than or Equal on a Peano System
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $a,b \in P$. One writes \[ a \le b \] exactly when \[ a<b \qquad\text{or}\qquad a=b. \]

dependencies.title: Strict Less Than on a Peano System

dependencies.title: Strict Less Than on a Peano System

dependencies.title: Addition on a Peano System
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For each $m \in P$, define the function \[ f_m : P \to P \] to be the unique function satisfying \[ f_m(1)=S(m) \qquad\text{and}\qquad \forall n \in P\;(f_m(S(n))=S(f_m(n))). \] For $m,n \in P$, define \[ m+n \coloneqq f_m(n). \]

dependencies.title: Induction Principle for a Peano System

dependencies.title: Subsequence
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. A \textbf{subsequence} of $(x_n)$ is a sequence $(x_{\sigma(k)})_{k\in\mathbb{N}}$, where $\sigma:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}$ is a strictly increasing index map.

dependencies.title: Sequence
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $X$ be a nonempty set. A \textbf{sequence} in $X$ is a function \[ x : \mathbb{N} \to X. \] The value $x(n)$ is written $x_n$ and called the \textbf{$n$-th term} of the sequence. The sequence itself is denoted $(x_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$, or simply $(x_n)$ when the index set is clear.

dependencies.title: Function
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $A$ and $B$ be sets. A \emph{function} from $A$ to $B$ is a relation $f \subseteq A \times B$ such that: \begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)] \item (\emph{Existence / left-total}) for every $a \in A$, there exists $b \in B$ with $(a,b) \in f$; \item (\emph{Uniqueness / right-unique}) if $(a,b_1) \in f$ and $(a,b_2) \in f$ then $b_1 = b_2$. \end{enumerate}...

dependencies.title: Cartesian Product — as foundation for relations
dependencies.statement_preview: The \emph{Cartesian product} of sets $A$ and $B$ is: \[ A \times B \;:=\; \{\, (a,b) \mid a \in A \text{ and } b \in B \,\}. \]

dependencies.title: Relation
dependencies.statement_preview: A \emph{relation} from $A$ to $B$ is any subset $R \subseteq A \times B$. If $(a,b) \in R$ we write $a \, R \, b$ and say ``$a$ is related to $b$.'' When $A = B$ we say $R$ is a \emph{relation on $A$}.

dependencies.title: Strictly Increasing Index Map
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $\sigma:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}$ be a function. The function $\sigma$ is a \textbf{strictly increasing index map} if \[ k<\ell \Longrightarrow \sigma(k)<\sigma(\ell) \qquad \text{for all } k,\ell\in\mathbb{N}. \]

dependencies.title: Strict Order Successor Characterization
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b \in P$, \[ a<b \Longleftrightarrow S(a) \le b. \]

dependencies.title: Less Than or Equal on a Peano System
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $a,b \in P$. One writes \[ a \le b \] exactly when \[ a<b \qquad\text{or}\qquad a=b. \]

dependencies.title: Strict Less Than on a Peano System
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $a,b \in P$. One writes \[ a<b \] exactly when there exists $c \in P$ such that \[ b=a+c. \]

dependencies.title: Addition Is Associative
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$, \[ (a+b)+c=a+(b+c). \]

dependencies.title: Addition Is Commutative
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b \in P$, \[ a+b=b+a. \]

dependencies.title: Addition Successor on the Right
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $m,n \in P$, \[ m+S(n)=S(m+n). \]

dependencies.title: Addition With $1$
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $m \in P$, \[ m+1=S(m). \]

dependencies.title: Every Element Is Either $1$ or a Successor
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $x \in P$, either $x=1$ or there exists $u \in P$ such that $S(u)=x$.

ancestor_tree.title: Cauchy Sequences Are Bounded
ancestor_tree.statement_preview: Every Cauchy real sequence is bounded.

dependencies.title: Bounded Sequence
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ is \textbf{bounded} exactly when there exists $M > 0$ such that $|x_n| \le M$ for every $n \in \mathbb{N}$.

dependencies.title: Axiom of Pairing
dependencies.statement_preview: For any two sets, there exists a set containing exactly those two sets. \[ \forall A \, \forall B \, \exists C \, \forall x \, (x \in C \leftrightarrow (x = A \lor x = B)). \]

dependencies.title: Axiom of Power Set
dependencies.statement_preview: For any set, there exists a set of all its subsets. \[ \forall A \, \exists P \, \forall x \, (x \in P \leftrightarrow x \subseteq A). \]

dependencies.title: Axiom Schema of Separation
dependencies.statement_preview: Given a set and a property, there exists a subset containing exactly the elements satisfying that property. For any formula $\varphi(x)$, \[ \forall A \, \exists B \, \forall x \, (x \in B \leftrightarrow (x \in A \land \varphi(x))). \]

dependencies.title: Ordered Pair
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $a$ and $b$ be sets. The \emph{ordered pair} $(a,b)$ is defined (Kuratowski) as \[ (a,b) \;:=\; \{\{a\}, \{a,b\}\}. \]

dependencies.statement_preview: Let $(x_n) \subseteq \mathbb{R}$. direct_dependencies.title: Pointwise Product of Sequences
direct_dependencies.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be real sequences. The \textbf{pointwise product} of $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ is the real sequence $(x_ny_n)$ defined by \[ (xy)_n := x_ny_n \qquad \text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}. \]

A \emph{function} from $A$ to $B$ is a relation $f \subseteq A \times B$ such that: \begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)] \item (\emph{Existence / left-total}) for every $a \in A$, there exists $b \in B$ with $(a,b) \in f$; \item (\emph{Uniqueness / right-unique}) if $(a,b_1) \in f$ and $(a,b_2) \in f$ then $b_1 = b_2$. \end{enumerate}...

transitive_dependencies.title: Language
transitive_dependencies.statement_preview: A \emph{first-order language} has two standard readings. As vocabulary, it is the same data as a signature, \[ \mathcal{L}=(\mathcal{C},\mathcal{F},\mathcal{R},\operatorname{ar}). \] As a formal language generated by a signature $\Sigma$, it is the set of well-formed strings built from the alphabet \[ A=\Sigma\cup V\cup\Lambda, \] where $V$ is a countable s...

transitive_dependencies.title: The Natural Numbers
transitive_dependencies.statement_preview: The natural numbers $\mathbb{N}$ are the canonical Peano system \[ (\mathbb{N},1,S), \] where $1 \in \mathbb{N}$ is the distinguished base element and $S:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}$ is the successor operation.

transitive_dependencies.title: Ordered Pair
transitive_dependencies.statement_preview: Let $a$ and $b$ be sets. The \emph{ordered pair} $(a,b)$ is defined (Kuratowski) as \[ (a,b) \;:=\; \{\{a\}, \{a,b\}\}. \]

transitive_dependencies.title: Peano System
transitive_dependencies.statement_preview: A triple $(P,S,1)$ is called a \emph{Peano system} exactly when $P$ is a set, $1 \in P$, $S : P \to P$ is a function, and the following axioms hold: \begin{enumerate} \item[\textbf{P1.}] $1 \in P$. \item[\textbf{P2.}] For every $n \in P$, $S(n) \in P$. \item[\textbf{P3.}] For every $n \in P$, $S(n) \neq 1$. \item[\textbf{P4.}] For all $m,n \in P$, if $S(m)=...

transitive_dependencies.title: Pointwise Product of Sequences
transitive_dependencies.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be real sequences. The \textbf{pointwise product} of $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ is the real sequence $(x_ny_n)$ defined by \[ (xy)_n := x_ny_n \qquad \text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}. \]

transitive_dependencies.title: Relation
transitive_dependencies.statement_preview: A \emph{relation} from $A$ to $B$ is any subset $R \subseteq A \times B$. If $(a,b) \in R$ we write $a \, R \, b$ and say ``$a$ is related to $b$.'' When $A = B$ we say $R$ is a \emph{relation on $A$}.

transitive_dependencies.title: Sequence
transitive_dependencies.statement_preview: Let $X$ be a nonempty set. A \textbf{sequence} in $X$ is a function \[ x : \mathbb{N} \to X. \] The value $x(n)$ is written $x_n$ and called the \textbf{$n$-th term} of the sequence. The sequence itself is denoted $(x_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$, or simply $(x_n)$ when the index set is clear.

transitive_dependencies.title: First-Order Signature
transitive_dependencies.statement_preview: A \emph{first-order signature} is a tuple \[ \Sigma=(\mathcal{C},\mathcal{F},\mathcal{R},\operatorname{ar}), \] where $\mathcal{C}$ is a set of constant symbols, $\mathcal{F}$ is a set of function symbols, $\mathcal{R}$ is a set of relation symbols, and \[ \operatorname{ar}:\mathcal{F}\cup\mathcal{R}\to \mathbb{Z}_{>0} \] assigns to each function or relatio...

transitive_dependencies.title: Subset
transitive_dependencies.statement_preview: Let $A$ and $B$ be sets. We say $A$ is a \emph{subset} of $B$, written $A \subseteq B$, if every element of $A$ is also an element of $B$: \[ A \subseteq B \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \forall x \, (x \in A \rightarrow x \in B). \]

transitive_dependencies.title: Universal Formula
transitive_dependencies.statement_preview: A \emph{universal formula} is a well-formed formula of the form \[ \forall x\,\varphi, \] where \(x\) is a variable and \(\varphi\) is a well-formed formula.

transitive_dependencies.title: Variable
transitive_dependencies.statement_preview: For a first-order language \(\mathcal L\), a \emph{variable} is a symbol drawn from the distinguished variable stock of \(\mathcal L\). Variables are usually written \[ x,y,z,x_0,x_1,x_2,\ldots . \]

transitive_dependencies.title: Cauchy Sequences Are Bounded
transitive_dependencies.statement_preview: Every Cauchy real sequence is bounded.

dependencies.title: Cartesian Product — as foundation for relations
dependencies.statement_preview: The \emph{Cartesian product} of sets $A$ and $B$ is: \[ A \times B \;:=\; \{\, (a,b) \mid a \in A \text{ and } b \in B \,\}. \]

dependencies.title: Axiom of Pairing
dependencies.statement_preview: For any two sets, there exists a set containing exactly those two sets. \[ \forall A \, \forall B \, \exists C \, \forall x \, (x \in C \leftrightarrow (x = A \lor x = B)). \]

dependencies.title: Axiom of Power Set
dependencies.statement_preview: For any set, there exists a set of all its subsets. \[ \forall A \, \exists P \, \forall x \, (x \in P \leftrightarrow x \subseteq A). \]

dependencies.title: Axiom Schema of Separation
dependencies.statement_preview: Given a set and a property, there exists a subset containing exactly the elements satisfying that property. For any formula $\varphi(x)$, \[ \forall A \, \exists B \, \forall x \, (x \in B \leftrightarrow (x \in A \land \varphi(x))). \]

dependencies.title: Bounded Sequence
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ is \textbf{bounded} exactly when there exists $M > 0$ such that $|x_n| \le M$ for every $n \in \mathbb{N}$.

nodes.title: Reciprocals of Cauchy Sequences
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a Cauchy real sequence. If there exist $c>0$ and
$N_0\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $|x_n|\ge c$ for every $n\ge N_0$, then
$(1/x_n)$ is Cauchy.
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a Cauchy real sequence. If there exist $c>0$ and
$N_0\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $|x_n|\ge c$ for every $n\ge N_0$, then
$(1/x_n)$ is Cauchy.

direct_dependencies.title: Cauchy Sequence
direct_dependencies.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ is a \textbf{Cauchy sequence} if \[ \forall \varepsilon>0 \;\exists N\in\mathbb{N}\;\forall m,n\ge N \;\bigl(|x_m-x_n|<\varepsilon\bigr). \]

direct_dependencies.title: Reciprocal Sequence
direct_dependencies.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence such that $x_n\neq 0$ for every $n\in\mathbb{N}$. The \textbf{reciprocal sequence} of $(x_n)$ is the real sequence $(1/x_n)$ defined by \[ \left(\frac{1}{x}\right)_n := \frac{1}{x_n} \qquad \text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}. \]

The sequence itself is denoted $(x_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$, or simply $(x_n)$ when the index set is clear.

dependencies.title: Cartesian Product — as foundation for relations
dependencies.statement_preview: The \emph{Cartesian product} of sets $A$ and $B$ is: \[ A \times B \;:=\; \{\, (a,b) \mid a \in A \text{ and } b \in B \,\}. \]

dependencies.title: Axiom of Pairing
dependencies.statement_preview: For any two sets, there exists a set containing exactly those two sets. \[ \forall A \, \forall B \, \exists C \, \forall x \, (x \in C \leftrightarrow (x = A \lor x = B)). \]

dependencies.title: Axiom of Power Set
dependencies.statement_preview: For any set, there exists a set of all its subsets. \[ \forall A \, \exists P \, \forall x \, (x \in P \leftrightarrow x \subseteq A). \]

dependencies.title: Axiom Schema of Separation
dependencies.statement_preview: Given a set and a property, there exists a subset containing exactly the elements satisfying that property. For any formula $\varphi(x)$, \[ \forall A \, \exists B \, \forall x \, (x \in B \leftrightarrow (x \in A \land \varphi(x))). \]

dependencies.title: Ordered Pair

dependencies.title: Relation

nodes.title: Quotients of Cauchy Sequences
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be Cauchy real sequences. If there exist $c>0$
and $N_0\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $|y_n|\ge c$ for every $n\ge N_0$,
then $(x_n/y_n)$ is Cauchy.
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be Cauchy real sequences. If there exist $c>0$
and $N_0\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $|y_n|\ge c$ for every $n\ge N_0$,
then $(x_n/y_n)$ is Cauchy.

direct_dependencies.title: Pointwise Quotient of Sequences
direct_dependencies.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be real sequences such that $y_n\neq 0$ for every $n\in\mathbb{N}$. The \textbf{pointwise quotient} of $(x_n)$ by $(y_n)$ is the real sequence $(x_n/y_n)$ defined by \[ \left(\frac{x}{y}\right)_n := \frac{x_n}{y_n} \qquad \text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}. \]

direct_dependencies.title: Products of Cauchy Sequences
direct_dependencies.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be Cauchy real sequences. Then $(x_ny_n)$ is Cauchy.

direct_dependencies.title: Reciprocals of Cauchy Sequences
direct_dependencies.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a Cauchy real sequence. If there exist $c>0$ and $N_0\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $|x_n|\ge c$ for every $n\ge N_0$, then $(1/x_n)$ is Cauchy.

transitive_dependencies.title: Bounded Sequence
transitive_dependencies.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ is \textbf{bounded} exactly when there exists $M > 0$ such that $|x_n| \le M$ for every $n \in \mathbb{N}$.

transitive_dependencies.title: Cauchy Sequence
transitive_dependencies.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ is a \textbf{Cauchy sequence} if \[ \forall \varepsilon>0 \;\exists N\in\mathbb{N}\;\forall m,n\ge N \;\bigl(|x_m-x_n|<\varepsilon\bigr). \]

transitive_dependencies.title: Pointwise Product of Sequences
transitive_dependencies.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be real sequences. The \textbf{pointwise product} of $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ is the real sequence $(x_ny_n)$ defined by \[ (xy)_n := x_ny_n \qquad \text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}. \]

transitive_dependencies.title: Variable
transitive_dependencies.statement_preview: For a first-order language \(\mathcal L\), a \emph{variable} is a symbol drawn from the distinguished variable stock of \(\mathcal L\). Variables are usually written \[ x,y,z,x_0,x_1,x_2,\ldots . \]

transitive_dependencies.title: Cauchy Sequences Are Bounded
transitive_dependencies.statement_preview: Every Cauchy real sequence is bounded.

A \emph{function} from $A$ to $B$ is a relation $f \subseteq A \times B$ such that: \begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)] \item (\emph{Existence / left-total}) for every $a \in A$, there exists $b \in B$ with $(a,b) \in f$; \item (\emph{Uniqueness / right-unique}) if $(a,b_1) \in f$ and $(a,b_2) \in f$ then $b_1 = b_2$. \end{enumerate}...

dependencies.title: Cartesian Product — as foundation for relations
dependencies.statement_preview: The \emph{Cartesian product} of sets $A$ and $B$ is: \[ A \times B \;:=\; \{\, (a,b) \mid a \in A \text{ and } b \in B \,\}. \]

dependencies.title: Axiom of Pairing
dependencies.statement_preview: For any two sets, there exists a set containing exactly those two sets. \[ \forall A \, \forall B \, \exists C \, \forall x \, (x \in C \leftrightarrow (x = A \lor x = B)). \]

dependencies.title: Axiom of Power Set
dependencies.statement_preview: For any set, there exists a set of all its subsets. \[ \forall A \, \exists P \, \forall x \, (x \in P \leftrightarrow x \subseteq A). \]

dependencies.title: Axiom Schema of Separation
dependencies.statement_preview: Given a set and a property, there exists a subset containing exactly the elements satisfying that property. For any formula $\varphi(x)$, \[ \forall A \, \exists B \, \forall x \, (x \in B \leftrightarrow (x \in A \land \varphi(x))). \]

dependencies.title: Ordered Pair
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $a$ and $b$ be sets. The \emph{ordered pair} $(a,b)$ is defined (Kuratowski) as \[ (a,b) \;:=\; \{\{a\}, \{a,b\}\}. \]

dependencies.title: Relation
dependencies.statement_preview: A \emph{relation} from $A$ to $B$ is any subset $R \subseteq A \times B$. If $(a,b) \in R$ we write $a \, R \, b$ and say ``$a$ is related to $b$.'' When $A = B$ we say $R$ is a \emph{relation on $A$}.

dependencies.title: Cartesian Product — as foundation for relations
dependencies.statement_preview: The \emph{Cartesian product} of sets $A$ and $B$ is: \[ A \times B \;:=\; \{\, (a,b) \mid a \in A \text{ and } b \in B \,\}. \]

dependencies.title: Subset
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $A$ and $B$ be sets. We say $A$ is a \emph{subset} of $B$, written $A \subseteq B$, if every element of $A$ is also an element of $B$: \[ A \subseteq B \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \forall x \, (x \in A \rightarrow x \in B). \]

dependencies.title: The Natural Numbers
dependencies.statement_preview: The natural numbers $\mathbb{N}$ are the canonical Peano system \[ (\mathbb{N},1,S), \] where $1 \in \mathbb{N}$ is the distinguished base element and $S:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}$ is the successor operation.

dependencies.title: Existence of a Peano System
dependencies.statement_preview: There exists a Peano system.

ancestor_tree.title: Products of Cauchy Sequences
ancestor_tree.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be Cauchy real sequences. Then $(x_ny_n)$ is Cauchy.

dependencies.title: Pointwise Product of Sequences
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be real sequences. The \textbf{pointwise product} of $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ is the real sequence $(x_ny_n)$ defined by \[ (xy)_n := x_ny_n \qquad \text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}. \]

dependencies.title: Sequence
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $X$ be a nonempty set. A \textbf{sequence} in $X$ is a function \[ x : \mathbb{N} \to X. \] The value $x(n)$ is written $x_n$ and called the \textbf{$n$-th term} of the sequence. The sequence itself is denoted $(x_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$, or simply $(x_n)$ when the index set is clear.

dependencies.title: Cauchy Sequences Are Bounded
dependencies.statement_preview: Every Cauchy real sequence is bounded.

dependencies.title: Bounded Sequence
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ is \textbf{bounded} exactly when there exists $M > 0$ such that $|x_n| \le M$ for every $n \in \mathbb{N}$.

dependencies.title: Cauchy Sequence
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ is a \textbf{Cauchy sequence} if \[ \forall \varepsilon>0 \;\exists N\in\mathbb{N}\;\forall m,n\ge N \;\bigl(|x_m-x_n|<\varepsilon\bigr). \]

ancestor_tree.title: Reciprocals of Cauchy Sequences
ancestor_tree.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a Cauchy real sequence. If there exist $c>0$ and $N_0\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $|x_n|\ge c$ for every $n\ge N_0$, then $(1/x_n)$ is Cauchy.

dependencies.title: Reciprocal Sequence
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence such that $x_n\neq 0$ for every $n\in\mathbb{N}$. The \textbf{reciprocal sequence} of $(x_n)$ is the real sequence $(1/x_n)$ defined by \[ \left(\frac{1}{x}\right)_n := \frac{1}{x_n} \qquad \text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}. \]

nodes.title: Scalar Multiples of Cauchy Sequences
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a Cauchy real sequence, and let $\alpha\in\mathbb{R}$.
Then $(\alpha x_n)$ is Cauchy.

transitive_dependencies.title: Sequence
transitive_dependencies.statement_preview: Let $X$ be a nonempty set. A \textbf{sequence} in $X$ is a function \[ x : \mathbb{N} \to X. \] The value $x(n)$ is written $x_n$ and called the \textbf{$n$-th term} of the sequence. The sequence itself is denoted $(x_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$, or simply $(x_n)$ when the index set is clear.

transitive_dependencies.title: First-Order Signature
transitive_dependencies.statement_preview: A \emph{first-order signature} is a tuple \[ \Sigma=(\mathcal{C},\mathcal{F},\mathcal{R},\operatorname{ar}), \] where $\mathcal{C}$ is a set of constant symbols, $\mathcal{F}$ is a set of function symbols, $\mathcal{R}$ is a set of relation symbols, and \[ \operatorname{ar}:\mathcal{F}\cup\mathcal{R}\to \mathbb{Z}_{>0} \] assigns to each function or relatio...

transitive_dependencies.title: Subset
transitive_dependencies.statement_preview: Let $A$ and $B$ be sets. We say $A$ is a \emph{subset} of $B$, written $A \subseteq B$, if every element of $A$ is also an element of $B$: \[ A \subseteq B \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \forall x \, (x \in A \rightarrow x \in B). \]

transitive_dependencies.title: Universal Formula
transitive_dependencies.statement_preview: A \emph{universal formula} is a well-formed formula of the form \[ \forall x\,\varphi, \] where \(x\) is a variable and \(\varphi\) is a well-formed formula.

transitive_dependencies.title: Variable
transitive_dependencies.statement_preview: For a first-order language \(\mathcal L\), a \emph{variable} is a symbol drawn from the distinguished variable stock of \(\mathcal L\). Variables are usually written \[ x,y,z,x_0,x_1,x_2,\ldots . \]

ancestor_tree.title: Cauchy Sequence
ancestor_tree.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ is a \textbf{Cauchy sequence} if \[ \forall \varepsilon>0 \;\exists N\in\mathbb{N}\;\forall m,n\ge N \;\bigl(|x_m-x_n|<\varepsilon\bigr). \]

dependencies.title: Sequence
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $X$ be a nonempty set. A \textbf{sequence} in $X$ is a function \[ x : \mathbb{N} \to X. \] The value $x(n)$ is written $x_n$ and called the \textbf{$n$-th term} of the sequence. The sequence itself is denoted $(x_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$, or simply $(x_n)$ when the index set is clear.

dependencies.title: Function
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $A$ and $B$ be sets. A \emph{function} from $A$ to $B$ is a relation $f \subseteq A \times B$ such that: \begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)] \item (\emph{Existence / left-total}) for every $a \in A$, there exists $b \in B$ with $(a,b) \in f$; \item (\emph{Uniqueness / right-unique}) if $(a,b_1) \in f$ and $(a,b_2) \in f$ then $b_1 = b_2$. \end{enumerate}...

dependencies.title: Cartesian Product — as foundation for relations
dependencies.statement_preview: The \emph{Cartesian product} of sets $A$ and $B$ is: \[ A \times B \;:=\; \{\, (a,b) \mid a \in A \text{ and } b \in B \,\}. \]

dependencies.title: Axiom of Pairing
dependencies.statement_preview: For any two sets, there exists a set containing exactly those two sets. \[ \forall A \, \forall B \, \exists C \, \forall x \, (x \in C \leftrightarrow (x = A \lor x = B)). \]

dependencies.title: Axiom of Power Set
dependencies.statement_preview: For any set, there exists a set of all its subsets. \[ \forall A \, \exists P \, \forall x \, (x \in P \leftrightarrow x \subseteq A). \]

dependencies.title: Axiom Schema of Separation
dependencies.statement_preview: Given a set and a property, there exists a subset containing exactly the elements satisfying that property. For any formula $\varphi(x)$, \[ \forall A \, \exists B \, \forall x \, (x \in B \leftrightarrow (x \in A \land \varphi(x))). \]

dependencies.title: Ordered Pair
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $a$ and $b$ be sets. The \emph{ordered pair} $(a,b)$ is defined (Kuratowski) as \[ (a,b) \;:=\; \{\{a\}, \{a,b\}\}. \]

dependencies.title: Axiom of Pairing
dependencies.statement_preview: For any two sets, there exists a set containing exactly those two sets. \[ \forall A \, \forall B \, \exists C \, \forall x \, (x \in C \leftrightarrow (x = A \lor x = B)). \]

dependencies.title: Relation
dependencies.statement_preview: A \emph{relation} from $A$ to $B$ is any subset $R \subseteq A \times B$. If $(a,b) \in R$ we write $a \, R \, b$ and say ``$a$ is related to $b$.'' When $A = B$ we say $R$ is a \emph{relation on $A$}.

dependencies.title: Cartesian Product — as foundation for relations
dependencies.statement_preview: The \emph{Cartesian product} of sets $A$ and $B$ is: \[ A \times B \;:=\; \{\, (a,b) \mid a \in A \text{ and } b \in B \,\}. \]

dependencies.title: The Natural Numbers
dependencies.statement_preview: The natural numbers $\mathbb{N}$ are the canonical Peano system \[ (\mathbb{N},1,S), \] where $1 \in \mathbb{N}$ is the distinguished base element and $S:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}$ is the successor operation.

dependencies.title: Existence of a Peano System
dependencies.statement_preview: There exists a Peano system.

nodes.title: Sums of Cauchy Sequences
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be Cauchy real sequences. Then $(x_n+y_n)$ is
Cauchy.

direct_dependencies.title: Pointwise Sum of Sequences
direct_dependencies.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be real sequences. The \textbf{pointwise sum} of $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ is the real sequence $(x_n+y_n)$ defined by \[ (x+y)_n := x_n+y_n \qquad \text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}. \]

ancestor_tree.title: Pointwise Sum of Sequences
ancestor_tree.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be real sequences. The \textbf{pointwise sum} of $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ is the real sequence $(x_n+y_n)$ defined by \[ (x+y)_n := x_n+y_n \qquad \text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}. \]

dependencies.title: Sequence
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $X$ be a nonempty set. A \textbf{sequence} in $X$ is a function \[ x : \mathbb{N} \to X. \] The value $x(n)$ is written $x_n$ and called the \textbf{$n$-th term} of the sequence. The sequence itself is denoted $(x_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$, or simply $(x_n)$ when the index set is clear.

dependencies.title: Function
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $A$ and $B$ be sets. A \emph{function} from $A$ to $B$ is a relation $f \subseteq A \times B$ such that: \begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)] \item (\emph{Existence / left-total}) for every $a \in A$, there exists $b \in B$ with $(a,b) \in f$; \item (\emph{Uniqueness / right-unique}) if $(a,b_1) \in f$ and $(a,b_2) \in f$ then $b_1 = b_2$. \end{enumerate}...

dependencies.title: Cartesian Product — as foundation for relations
dependencies.statement_preview: The \emph{Cartesian product} of sets $A$ and $B$ is: \[ A \times B \;:=\; \{\, (a,b) \mid a \in A \text{ and } b \in B \,\}. \]

dependencies.title: Axiom of Pairing
dependencies.statement_preview: For any two sets, there exists a set containing exactly those two sets. \[ \forall A \, \forall B \, \exists C \, \forall x \, (x \in C \leftrightarrow (x = A \lor x = B)). \]

dependencies.title: Axiom of Power Set
dependencies.statement_preview: For any set, there exists a set of all its subsets. \[ \forall A \, \exists P \, \forall x \, (x \in P \leftrightarrow x \subseteq A). \]

dependencies.title: Axiom Schema of Separation
dependencies.statement_preview: Given a set and a property, there exists a subset containing exactly the elements satisfying that property. For any formula $\varphi(x)$, \[ \forall A \, \exists B \, \forall x \, (x \in B \leftrightarrow (x \in A \land \varphi(x))). \]

dependencies.title: Ordered Pair
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $a$ and $b$ be sets. The \emph{ordered pair} $(a,b)$ is defined (Kuratowski) as \[ (a,b) \;:=\; \{\{a\}, \{a,b\}\}. \]

dependencies.title: Axiom of Pairing

dependencies.title: Relation

direct_dependencies.title: Pointwise Difference of Sequences
direct_dependencies.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be real sequences. The \textbf{pointwise difference} of $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ is the real sequence $(x_n-y_n)$ defined by \[ (x-y)_n := x_n-y_n \qquad \text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}. \]

transitive_dependencies.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ is a \textbf{Cauchy sequence} if \[ \forall \varepsilon>0 \;\exists N\in\mathbb{N}\;\forall m,n\ge N \;\bigl(|x_m-x_n|<\varepsilon\bigr). \]

transitive_dependencies.title: Variable

A \emph{function} from $A$ to $B$ is a relation $f \subseteq A \times B$ such that: \begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)] \item (\emph{Existence / left-total}) for every $a \in A$, there exists $b \in B$ with $(a,b) \in f$; \item (\emph{Uniqueness / right-unique}) if $(a,b_1) \in f$ and $(a,b_2) \in f$ then $b_1 = b_2$. \end{enumerate}...

dependencies.title: Cartesian Product — as foundation for relations
dependencies.statement_preview: The \emph{Cartesian product} of sets $A$ and $B$ is: \[ A \times B \;:=\; \{\, (a,b) \mid a \in A \text{ and } b \in B \,\}. \]

dependencies.title: Axiom of Pairing
dependencies.statement_preview: For any two sets, there exists a set containing exactly those two sets. \[ \forall A \, \forall B \, \exists C \, \forall x \, (x \in C \leftrightarrow (x = A \lor x = B)). \]

dependencies.title: Axiom of Power Set
dependencies.statement_preview: For any set, there exists a set of all its subsets. \[ \forall A \, \exists P \, \forall x \, (x \in P \leftrightarrow x \subseteq A). \]

dependencies.title: Axiom Schema of Separation
dependencies.statement_preview: Given a set and a property, there exists a subset containing exactly the elements satisfying that property. For any formula $\varphi(x)$, \[ \forall A \, \exists B \, \forall x \, (x \in B \leftrightarrow (x \in A \land \varphi(x))). \]

dependencies.title: Ordered Pair
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $a$ and $b$ be sets. The \emph{ordered pair} $(a,b)$ is defined (Kuratowski) as \[ (a,b) \;:=\; \{\{a\}, \{a,b\}\}. \]

dependencies.title: Relation
dependencies.statement_preview: A \emph{relation} from $A$ to $B$ is any subset $R \subseteq A \times B$. If $(a,b) \in R$ we write $a \, R \, b$ and say ``$a$ is related to $b$.'' When $A = B$ we say $R$ is a \emph{relation on $A$}.

dependencies.title: Cartesian Product — as foundation for relations
dependencies.statement_preview: The \emph{Cartesian product} of sets $A$ and $B$ is: \[ A \times B \;:=\; \{\, (a,b) \mid a \in A \text{ and } b \in B \,\}. \]

dependencies.title: Subset
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $A$ and $B$ be sets. We say $A$ is a \emph{subset} of $B$, written $A \subseteq B$, if every element of $A$ is also an element of $B$: \[ A \subseteq B \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \forall x \, (x \in A \rightarrow x \in B). \]

dependencies.title: The Natural Numbers
dependencies.statement_preview: The natural numbers $\mathbb{N}$ are the canonical Peano system \[ (\mathbb{N},1,S), \] where $1 \in \mathbb{N}$ is the distinguished base element and $S:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}$ is the successor operation.

dependencies.title: Peano System
dependencies.statement_preview: A triple $(P,S,1)$ is called a \emph{Peano system} exactly when $P$ is a set, $1 \in P$, $S : P \to P$ is a function, and the following axioms hold: \begin{enumerate} \item[\textbf{P1.}] $1 \in P$. \item[\textbf{P2.}] For every $n \in P$, $S(n) \in P$. \item[\textbf{P3.}] For every $n \in P$, $S(n) \neq 1$. \item[\textbf{P4.}] For all $m,n \in P$, if $S(m)=...

dependencies.title: Existence of a Peano System
dependencies.statement_preview: There exists a Peano system.

dependencies.title: Scalar Multiple of a Sequence
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence, and let $\alpha\in\mathbb{R}$. The \textbf{scalar multiple} $\alpha(x_n)$ is the real sequence $(\alpha x_n)$ defined by \[ (\alpha x)_n := \alpha x_n \qquad \text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}. \]

dependencies.title: Sequence
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $X$ be a nonempty set. A \textbf{sequence} in $X$ is a function \[ x : \mathbb{N} \to X. \] The value $x(n)$ is written $x_n$ and called the \textbf{$n$-th term} of the sequence. The sequence itself is denoted $(x_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$, or simply $(x_n)$ when the index set is clear.

dependencies.title: Cauchy Sequence
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ is a \textbf{Cauchy sequence} if \[ \forall \varepsilon>0 \;\exists N\in\mathbb{N}\;\forall m,n\ge N \;\bigl(|x_m-x_n|<\varepsilon\bigr). \]

nodes.title: Linear Combinations of Cauchy Sequences
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be Cauchy real sequences, and let
$\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb{R}$. Then $(\alpha x_n+\beta y_n)$ is Cauchy.
nodes.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be Cauchy real sequences, and let
$\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb{R}$. Then $(\alpha x_n+\beta y_n)$ is Cauchy.

direct_dependencies.title: Linear Combination of Real Sequences
direct_dependencies.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be real sequences, and let $\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb{R}$. The real linear combination $\alpha(x_n)+\beta(y_n)$ is the real sequence $(\alpha x_n+\beta y_n)$ defined by \[ (\alpha x+\beta y)_n := \alpha x_n+\beta y_n \qquad \text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}. \]

direct_dependencies.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be Cauchy real sequences. Then $(x_n+y_n)$ is Cauchy.

transitive_dependencies.title: Universal Formula
transitive_dependencies.statement_preview: A \emph{universal formula} is a well-formed formula of the form \[ \forall x\,\varphi, \] where \(x\) is a variable and \(\varphi\) is a well-formed formula.

transitive_dependencies.title: Variable
transitive_dependencies.statement_preview: For a first-order language \(\mathcal L\), a \emph{variable} is a symbol drawn from the distinguished variable stock of \(\mathcal L\). Variables are usually written \[ x,y,z,x_0,x_1,x_2,\ldots . \]

dependencies.statement_preview: Given a set and a property, there exists a subset containing exactly the elements satisfying that property. For any formula $\varphi(x)$, \[ \forall A \, \exists B \, \forall x \, (x \in B \leftrightarrow (x \in A \land \varphi(x))). \]

dependencies.title: Ordered Pair
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $a$ and $b$ be sets. The \emph{ordered pair} $(a,b)$ is defined (Kuratowski) as \[ (a,b) \;:=\; \{\{a\}, \{a,b\}\}. \]

dependencies.title: Relation
dependencies.statement_preview: A \emph{relation} from $A$ to $B$ is any subset $R \subseteq A \times B$. If $(a,b) \in R$ we write $a \, R \, b$ and say ``$a$ is related to $b$.'' When $A = B$ we say $R$ is a \emph{relation on $A$}.

dependencies.title: Cartesian Product — as foundation for relations
dependencies.statement_preview: The \emph{Cartesian product} of sets $A$ and $B$ is: \[ A \times B \;:=\; \{\, (a,b) \mid a \in A \text{ and } b \in B \,\}. \]

dependencies.title: Subset
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $A$ and $B$ be sets. We say $A$ is a \emph{subset} of $B$, written $A \subseteq B$, if every element of $A$ is also an element of $B$: \[ A \subseteq B \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \forall x \, (x \in A \rightarrow x \in B). \]

dependencies.title: The Natural Numbers
dependencies.statement_preview: The natural numbers $\mathbb{N}$ are the canonical Peano system \[ (\mathbb{N},1,S), \] where $1 \in \mathbb{N}$ is the distinguished base element and $S:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}$ is the successor operation.

dependencies.title: Existence of a Peano System
dependencies.statement_preview: There exists a Peano system.

dependencies.title: Scalar Multiple of a Sequence
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence, and let $\alpha\in\mathbb{R}$. The \textbf{scalar multiple} $\alpha(x_n)$ is the real sequence $(\alpha x_n)$ defined by \[ (\alpha x)_n := \alpha x_n \qquad \text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}. \]

dependencies.title: Sequence
dependencies.statement_preview: Let $X$ be a nonempty set. A \textbf{sequence} in $X$ is a function \[ x : \mathbb{N} \to X. \] The value $x(n)$ is written $x_n$ and called the \textbf{$n$-th term} of the sequence. The sequence itself is denoted $(x_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$, or simply $(x_n)$ when the index set is clear.
